title: Back to Gomer
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 03-23-25 -PM- Back to Gomer - Hosea 3 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: God Illustrates What He Will Do
subpoints: God knows that a future promise without a short-term illustration may not be enough to provide hope — so he gives Hosea a living illustration
'Go again, love a woman who is loved by a lover and is committing adultery, just like the love of the Lord for the children of Israel' (v.1)
Five things to see: the commands, the condition of Gomer, the cost, the consequence, and the consummation

outline.title: 1. The Commands: Go and Love
subpoints: Two imperatives in the original: 'go' and 'love' — God is not telling Hosea to receive a repentant Gomer who is returning; he is to go, pursue, and seek her where she is
Love is commanded — it is not an uncontrollable emotion but an act of the will; God commands it and expects Hosea to be able to obey
The culture says we 'fall into' and 'fall out of' love — that is not how Scripture defines love; God sees love as something we can learn and grow in
Implication for marriage: 'I don't love my spouse anymore' and 'my spouse is unlovable' are not biblical excuses; Hosea is told to love the most unlovable person imaginable

outline.title: 2. The Condition: Gomer's Unlovability
subpoints: Gomer is actively committing adultery — her many lovers are with her; there is nothing lovable about her from a human perspective
The analogy: just as Gomer, so Israel — who looks to other gods and loves the raisin cakes of the pagans; preferring the delicacies of false gods over the God who loves her
Romans 3:11: 'there is none who seeks after God'; Romans 5:8-10: 'while we were still sinners... when we were enemies, Christ died for us'
We too, in our natural state, are more in love with the raisin cakes of false gods than with the God who loves us

outline.title: 3. The Cost: The Price of Redemption
subpoints: V.2: Hosea paid 15 shekels of silver plus grain — totaling approximately 30 shekels, the price of a slave (Exodus 21:32)
Gomer had apparently become enslaved, possibly through her behavior; Hosea redeemed her — purchased her back
Redemption is purchase at a price; 1 Corinthians 6:20: 'You were bought at a price' — not 30 shekels of silver but the blood of the Lord Jesus Christ
Interesting parallel: 30 pieces of silver is also exactly what Judas received for betraying Jesus — the price of a slave was used to hand over the one whose death would redeem slaves to sin

outline.title: 4. The Consequence: A Season of Abstinence
subpoints: V.3: 'You shall stay with me many days. You shall not play the harlot, nor shall you have a man; so too will I be toward you' — both will live celibate for a season
This is not normal marriage (1 Corinthians 7:3-5) but a symbolic imposed period; possibly connected to Deuteronomy 21 (a month before consummating marriage with a captive wife)
V.4: the symbolism is explicit — Israel will abide many days without king, prince, sacrifice, pillar, ephod, or teraphim; the captivity period when the means of grace are removed
Amos 8:11-12: the famine of the Word of God — when people neglect the Word, God may take it away; one of the punishments of captivity is removal of the ability to observe the Sabbath
Warning: believers who resist the Sabbath are choosing the condition of curse; absence of a Sabbath in Scripture is a sign of God's judgment

outline.title: 5. The Consummation: Return and the Greater David
subpoints: V.5: afterward the children of Israel shall return and seek the Lord their God and 'David their King' — striking because Hosea is prophesying to the northern kingdom which had abandoned David long ago
This is not David the son of Jesse but the greater David — the Messiah, the Son of God; the northern kingdom will embrace the ultimate David
In the latter days they will fear the Lord and his goodness — they will stand in awe of God and his goodness in the ultimate Son of David
The judgments God brings into our lives are designed to bring us back to himself so we can live in awe of him and his goodness
For those who have embraced the greater David (the Lord Jesus Christ), we stand in awe of God because we know for certain: he is good and does good

summary: An afternoon sermon on Hosea 3, where God commands Hosea to go and pursue the adulterous Gomer as a living illustration of God's pursuit of Israel. Five elements of the passage are traced: (1) the commands — 'go and love'; love is an act of will that can be commanded, not an uncontrollable emotion; (2) the condition — Gomer is actively unfaithful, as Israel pursues foreign gods (raisin cakes of pagans); we too prefer the false gods to the true God (Romans 3:11; 5:8-10); (3) the cost — Hosea paid roughly 30 shekels to redeem Gomer from slavery, the price of a slave (Exodus 21:32); parallel to 30 pieces of silver paid to betray Jesus; 1 Corinthians 6:20 — 'you were bought at a price'; (4) the consequence — a season of celibacy symbolizing Israel's captivity period without king, sacrifice, or the means of grace; Amos 8:11-12 warns of the famine of the Word; the absence of a Sabbath is a mark of God's judgment; (5) the consummation — afterward Israel will return and seek the Lord and 'David their King' — the greater David, the Messiah; God's judgments are designed to bring us back to himself so we may live in awe of his goodness.
transcript:
## Introduction

In the second chapter of Hosea, God has spoken of great things for his people through the new covenant — better in every aspect than anything his people had ever experienced. But God knows how humans function. He knows that a future promise without a short-term illustration or a short-term taste of what is to come may not be enough to sustain hope or motivate his people. So he gives Hosea a living illustration. He tells Hosea to go and get Gomer — and in that pursuit, to illustrate what God himself will do for Israel.

I want us to see five things in this passage: the commands God gives, the condition Gomer is in when Hosea pursues her, the cost of pursuing her, the consequence of the whole arrangement, and finally the consummation — where all of this is going.

## 1. The Commands: Go and Love

Verse 1: *Then the LORD said to me, 'Go again, love a woman who is loved by a lover and is committing adultery...'*

Two imperatives in the original: *go* and *love*. God is not telling Hosea to receive a repentant Gomer who is on her way home. He is not saying, 'She's coming back — take her.' He is saying: *go*. Go to her. Pursue her. Seek her out where she is.

And he commands Hosea to *love* her. It is not enough for Hosea to pursue her — he is to love her. And notice that God expects Hosea to be able to obey the command to love. Hosea cannot say, 'But Lord, I don't really feel it anymore.' The expectation is that he will go, pursue, seek, and love.

We are often told that love is something we cannot control — that you fall into love and you fall out of love. But that is not how God speaks of love. God sees love as an act of the will, not an uncontrollable emotion. It is something we can be commanded to do. It is something we can learn and grow in.

This has profound implications for marriage. When someone says, 'I don't love my spouse anymore' — or 'My spouse is unlovable, so I can't love them' — those statements do not hold up biblically. Gomer is about as unlovable as a person can be. And God says to Hosea: go. Love her.

## 2. The Condition: Gomer's Unlovability

Gomer is actively committing adultery. Her many lovers are with her. There is nothing lovable about her from a human perspective. And this is the woman Hosea is supposed to pursue and love.

The analogy is made explicit in the second half of verse 1: *just like the love of the LORD for the children of Israel, who look to other gods and love the raisin cakes of the pagans.* Israel is more interested in the raisin cakes of the false gods than in the God who loves her. She prefers the delicacies of idolatry to the true God.

And this is us. It is easy to look at Israel in the Old Testament and shake your head. But the Bible describes us the same way. Romans 3:11: *There is none who understands; there is none who seeks after God.* In our natural state, we do not pursue God. Romans 5:8-10: *While we were still sinners, Christ died for us... when we were enemies, we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son.*

We are the Gomer. God is the Hosea. And God pursues us with saving love even when we are not pursuing him.

## 3. The Cost: Redemption at a Price

Verse 2: *So I bought her for myself for fifteen shekels of silver, and one and a half homers of barley.*

Adding the grain and silver together, Hosea paid approximately thirty shekels of silver for Gomer — the price set in Exodus 21:32 for a slave. Gomer had apparently become enslaved through her behavior. Whatever the precise arrangement, Hosea redeemed her — he purchased her back.

Redemption is purchase at a price. 1 Corinthians 6:20: *You were bought at a price. Therefore glorify God in your body and in your spirit, which are God's.* We too have been redeemed — but not for thirty shekels of silver. The price was the blood of the Lord Jesus Christ.

There is a striking parallel worth noting: thirty pieces of silver is exactly the amount Judas received for betraying Jesus. The price of a slave — the price Hosea paid to redeem Gomer — is the price that was paid to hand over the one who would shed his blood to redeem us from slavery to sin.

## 4. The Consequence: A Season of Absence

Verse 3: *And I said to her, 'You shall stay with me many days; you shall not play the harlot, nor shall you have a man — so too will I be toward you.'*

Both Hosea and Gomer will live celibate for a season. This is not normal marriage — 1 Corinthians 7:3-5 makes clear that a husband and wife are not to deprive one another except by mutual consent for a season. This is an imposed absence, symbolic in nature. There may also be a connection to Deuteronomy 21, which required a month of living together before consummating a marriage with a captive wife.

Verse 4 gives the explanation: *For the children of Israel shall abide many days without king or prince, without sacrifice or sacred pillar, without ephod or teraphim.* The period of celibacy symbolizes the captivity — the time when the means of grace will not be readily available to the people of God.

Amos 8:11-12 speaks of a famine in the land — not a famine of bread or water but of the Word of God. When people do not avail themselves of the means of grace, God may remove them: when you want them, you will not find them.

One of the punishments of captivity was the removal of the ability to observe the Sabbath. This is a warning worth hearing: the absence of a Sabbath in Scripture is a mark of God's judgment. To resist and avoid the Christian Sabbath is to choose the condition of the cursed.

## 5. The Consummation: The Greater David

Verse 5: *Afterward the children of Israel shall return and seek the LORD their God and David their king. They shall fear the LORD and his goodness in the latter days.*

The reason for the captivity, for the absence, for the period of discipline — is to prepare Israel to return to the Lord. The pain was purposeful. The absence was designed.

And notice what they return to: *David their king*. This should strike us. Hosea is prophesying to the northern kingdom — which had abandoned David long ago, when Jeroboam led ten tribes away to establish a separate state. But after God's purifying judgment has done its work, they embrace David.

This is not David the son of Jesse. This is the greater David — the Messiah, the Son of God. The whole of Hosea, the whole of the prophets, moves toward that day of the ultimate Son of David.

In the latter days they will *fear the LORD and his goodness*. They will stand in awe of God and in awe of how good he is to them in the ultimate David.

Brothers and sisters: the judgments God brings into our lives are designed to bring us back to himself — so that we can live in awe of him and his goodness. And for those who have embraced the greater David, the Lord Jesus Christ, we already stand in that awe. We know for a fact: he is good, and he does good.

Father, thank you for your Word. Embed it in our hearts that we might live according to it. In Jesus' name, amen.
